Event: Suggests using the Ring as a weapon for Gondor at the Council of Elrond
Explanation: Obsession with using the Ring endangers himself and the wider mission with no real chance of benefit given what he knows of its corruption.

Event: States “If this is indeed the will of the council, then Gondor will see it done.” and joins the quest
Explanation: Commitment to the council’s decision aids the mission while aligning with his duty to Gondor.

Event: Treats Aragorn with prickly hostility while others get normal treatment
Explanation: Antagonizing Aragorn strains Fellowship unity and offers Boromir no tangible gain.

Event: Defends Faramir verbally against Denethor’s cruel detractions
Explanation: Risks displeasing his father but emotionally supports and validates his brother.

Event: Openly calls out Denethor for mistreating Faramir
Explanation: Confrontation may cost him favor but pushes against harmful parenting for Faramir’s sake.

Event: Leads Gondor’s army to victory as its champion
Explanation: Successful leadership bolsters his status and directly benefits his people militarily and morally.

Event: Keeps his victory speech short so there’s “more time for drinking.”
Explanation: Maintains camaraderie and morale while modestly enjoying celebration.

Event: Advocates repeatedly for bringing the Ring to Gondor despite warnings
Explanation: Persistence in a known-doomed strategy undermines trust and mission success with little realistic upside.

Event: Remains the only one to keep raising the “use the Ring” idea after black speech warning
Explanation: Ignoring clear supernatural warning increases risk of catastrophe for all with no concrete benefit realized.

Event: Treats Hobbits supportively, teaching Merry and Pippin swordplay
Explanation: He gains affection and capable allies while the Hobbits gain crucial skills.

Event: Defends Merry and Pippin first when Uruk-hai attack
Explanation: He endangers himself severely to shield them, substantially benefiting them at high personal cost.

Event: Comforts and restrains Gimli while he mourns in Moria
Explanation: He expends emotional effort to steady a comrade, strengthening group cohesion.

Event: Comforts Frodo after Gandalf’s death in Lothlórien
Explanation: Supports Frodo’s morale, indirectly aiding the quest while also deepening mutual trust.

Event: Tells Aragorn “Give them a moment, for pity’s sake!” to allow grieving
Explanation: He advocates rest and emotional processing, which likely improves resilience for everyone including himself.

Event: Implores Gandalf to turn back from Caradhras for the Hobbits’ safety
Explanation: He both protects vulnerable members and nudges path toward his strategic preference without harming them.

Event: Holds disregard for Elves, Dwarves, and Aragorn’s claim early on
Explanation: Prejudice harms cooperation and brings him no concrete gain, even undermining his own aims.

Event: Eventually overcomes his racism toward Elves and Dwarves and accepts Aragorn
Explanation: Revising his view enhances alliance strength and thus his own side’s chances.

Event: Grabs the cave troll’s chain with Aragorn to pull it from Sam
Explanation: He risks injury but successfully protects Sam, strengthening the Fellowship’s survival.

Event: Continues fighting after being slammed into a wall by the troll
Explanation: Pushing through injury helps defeat the enemy and defend the group.

Event: Blows the Horn of Gondor to draw Uruks away from Frodo and others
Explanation: He knowingly makes himself a focus of attack, sacrificing safety to save Frodo and relieve comrades.

Event: Makes a last stand against dozens of Uruk-hai to protect Merry and Pippin
Explanation: He effectively trades his life to buy them time, greatly aiding them while fatally harming himself.

Event: Keeps fighting after two arrows to the chest
Explanation: Perseveres through mortal wounds to continue shielding the Hobbits at enormous personal cost.

Event: Faces Lurtz defiantly, staring him down before the killing blow
Explanation: Though it doesn’t alter outcomes, it preserves his dignity and morale without affecting others much.

Event: Falls into despair over his failures as he dies
Explanation: His self-condemnation harms his own peace of mind but doesn’t materially affect others.

Event: Regains his resolve after Aragorn’s reassurance
Explanation: Recovering his sense of honor comforts both him and Aragorn and strengthens Aragorn’s resolve.

Event: Tells Aragorn he would have been proud to recognize him as king
Explanation: His dying endorsement emotionally empowers Aragorn and gives Boromir some closure.

Event: Initially dismisses Aragorn as an unworthy Ranger with an empty claim
Explanation: Undermining a key ally sours relations without improving Boromir’s position.

Event: Acknowledges Aragorn as “my brother, my captain, my king” as he dies
Explanation: Affirmation encourages Aragorn to embrace leadership, benefiting Gondor and honoring Boromir.

Event: Tries to get Aragorn to open up in private and speak emotionally
Explanation: Attempts at emotional connection can deepen trust and teamwork, also giving Boromir relational support.

Event: Tries to take the Ring from Frodo by force in the woods
Explanation: He gains nothing (Frodo escapes) while shattering trust and splintering the Fellowship, endangering all.

Event: Curses the Hobbits during his Ring-obsessed confrontation
Explanation: Hostility damages relationships and brings him no compensating benefit.

Event: Experiences “My God, What Have I Done?” remorse after attacking Frodo
Explanation: Realization pains him but starts moral recovery and indirectly helps reorient him to others’ good.

Event: Is motivated by guilt to defend Merry and Pippin afterward
Explanation: His self-punishing atonement leads him to die for their protection, heavily favoring them over himself.

Event: Sheds tears over his attempted theft of the Ring
Explanation: His grief hurts him but marks sincere repentance and a turn back toward concern for others.

Event: Develops a protective “big brother” instinct for Merry and Pippin
Explanation: His affection drives training and defense that benefit them and give him meaningful bonds.

Event: Shows open affection and hugs for Faramir despite their father
Explanation: Affection strengthens Faramir emotionally and gives Boromir a healthier relationship.

Event: Argues that Gondor has done the fighting while others sat out
Explanation: His pointed criticism risks friction but prompts Aragorn’s later commitment to defend Gondor.

Event: Maintains a close, supportive sibling relationship with Faramir
Explanation: Mutual emotional and practical support benefits both brothers and their effectiveness.

Event: Refuses to enjoy being Denethor’s favorite and resents the favoritism
Explanation: His stance hurts his standing with Denethor but morally backs fairness towards Faramir.

Event: Speaks with strong conviction that the Ring could be used for good
Explanation: His misplaced confidence encourages a path that would corrupt Gondor and himself with no success shown.

Event: Lets his desire for the Ring grow into sanity slippage
Explanation: His deteriorating judgment endangers the quest and his own mind without any realized gain.

Event: Attempts to comfort Frodo in Lórien: “Gandalf’s death was not in vain…”
Explanation: His consolation supports Frodo’s resolve and gives Boromir a constructive role after trauma.

Event: Puts his hand on Gimli’s shoulder and restrains him gently outside Moria
Explanation: He provides physical and emotional grounding without harming himself.

Event: Implores Aragorn while dying: “They took the little ones!”
Explanation: He uses his last strength to direct Aragorn’s efforts toward saving Merry and Pippin, sacrificing focus on himself.

Event: Aragorn promises Boromir he will not let the White City fall
Explanation: The vow comforts Boromir and commits Aragorn to actions that will help Gondor.

Event: Aragorn takes Boromir’s bracers as a keepsake and reminder
Explanation: The memento strengthens Aragorn’s sense of duty toward Gondor, aiding future actions.

Event: Faramir returns Boromir’s broken horn to Denethor
Explanation: Returning the horn informs and emotionally impacts Denethor, shaping later decisions, without harming Faramir.

Event: Denethor keeps Boromir’s broken horn as a keepsake
Explanation: It preserves his connection to Boromir and may indirectly influence his later behavior, with no clear harm.
